Moses stands before the people of Israel with a question that cuts to the heart of everything: Do you love the LORD your God? Not eventually. Not when it feels convenient. Now. Always. He doesn't begin with abstract theology but with memory—he summons them to recall what their own eyes have witnessed. The plagues that devastated Egypt. The Red Sea collapsing over Pharaoh's army. The earth swallowing Dathan and Abiram alive. These were not stories passed down; these were events the generation standing before Moses had seen with their own eyes. They had watched God's power displayed, not as distant history but as lived reality. This is the foundation Moses lays: obedience to God's commands must rest on the bedrock of what you have actually seen God do. You cannot claim ignorance. You cannot say His character is unknown to you. Your eyes have seen His greatness.

Yet Moses also makes clear that the land they are about to enter will demand something different from what they knew in Egypt or in the wilderness wanderings. The land ahead depends on rain from heaven, not on human irrigation and effort. It is a land that requires God's constant care—literally, the eyes of the LORD your God are always on it. This stunning image reveals that possession is not the end of God's involvement; it is the beginning of a new kind of dependence. If they obey and love the LORD with all their heart and soul, He will send rain in season and they will eat and be satisfied. But if they turn to other gods—if they forget what they have seen and embrace what they have not known—God's anger will kindle, the heavens will shut, and they will perish from the very land He is giving them. Blessing and curse are not arbitrary; they flow directly from faithfulness or unfaithfulness to God.

Moses then gives them a radical charge: these words are not to be heard once and forgotten. They must be fixed in your hearts and minds. Tied as reminders on your hands. Bound on your foreheads. Taught to your children in every moment—sitting at home, walking along the road, lying down, getting up. Written on the doorposts of your houses and gates. This is not mere repetition or religious decoration; it is a total reorientation of life around God's word. Every action, every threshold, every conversation becomes an occasion to remember who God is and what He demands. The long life in the land—the multiplication of days for them and their children—depends on this vigilance, this refusal to let God's word slip from consciousness.

The chapter closes where it began: with a choice set starkly before them. Not a hidden choice. Not one they must guess at. A blessing if you obey. A curse if you turn aside to other gods. The mountains Gerizim and Ebal will become living monuments to this decision, with the blessings and curses pronounced on them as the people cross the Jordan. There is no middle ground here, no comfortable neutrality. Moses is telling them that every day in the land ahead will be shaped by whether they remember what they have seen, whether they love God with all their heart, and whether they teach their children to do the same. The inheritance is not automatic. It is conditional on obedience. And obedience is not born from duty alone but from the deep recognition of who God is—a God whose mighty hand and outstretched arm have already done the impossible on their behalf.
